text: Abstract: Background: Dietary medicinal plants are the subject of great attention nowadays in the field of alternative medicine due to their therapeutic role in preventing and treating many diseases. Aim: The main objective of this investigation is to isolate the polyphenols from local medicinal plants extracts that are used clinically in Saudi Arabia i.e., Mentha longifolia, M. arvensis, Tinospora cordifolia, Cymbopogon citratus, Foeniculum vulgare, Cassia absus, Camellia sinensis, Trachyspermum ammi, and to study their antioxidant, cytotoxic, antibacterial and enzyme inhibition activities using isolated polyphenols. Methods: The antioxidant activity of medicinal plants polyphenolic extracts (MPPE) was confirmed using DPPH, Superoxide radical, Hydroxyl radical (OH.) scavenging and Nitric oxide (NO.) methods. The bioactivity towards enzyme inhibition and the anticancer activities were assessed using enzyme methods and MTT assay, respectively. In addition, the antibacterial activity of the tested medicinal plants extracts was investigated using the agar streak method. Results: The majority of the tested medicinal plants extracts have shown a remarkable scavenging activities towards DPPH, OH., NO., and superoxide free radicals. TheMPPE with higher scavenging activities contained significantly higher amounts of total polyphenol and flavonoids. The identified polyphenols by UHPLC profile revealed that twenty-five polyphenol complexes in eight medicinal plant extracts which were categorized as phenolic acids, flavonoids, and alkaloids. The majority of the identified polyphenols such as 3-Feroylquinic acid (1,302 mg/L), rosmarinic acid, vanillic acid, chlorogenic acid, p-coumaric acid, ferulic acid, gallic acid, catechin, luteolin, 7-O -neohesperideside, quercetin 3,7-O -glucoside, hesperidin, rutin, quercetin, and caffeine were detected in the higher quantities in Camellia sinensis as they were ranging from (560–780 mg/L) and up to 116% more than their amounts in Mentha longifolia, Cassia absus, and other medicinal plants. In contrast, Tinospora cordifolia was rich in alkaloids content whereas Trachyspermum ammi had the lowest content of these polyphenols. The majority of the tested medicinal plants extracts at the concentration of (500 μg/mL) exhibited strong cytotoxic activity against Caco-2 cell lines using the MTT assay as the polyphenolic extracts of Trachyspermum ammi and Cymbopogon citratus had maximum cytotoxicity. On the other hand, the enzyme inhibition assays revealed that Medicial plants polyphenolic extracts (MPPE) of Mentha arvensis, Camellia sinensis, and Foeniculum vulgare showed a significant enzyme inhibition activity whereas Camellia sinensis had minor inhibition activity towards α-amylase. Furthermore, the antibiotic assay revealed that Foeniculum vulgare and Camellia sinensis exhibited considerable antibacterial activity towards Staphylococcus aureus, Bacillus cereus, Escherichia coli, and Salmonella enterica. Discussion The principal component analysis demonstrated clear separation among medicinal plants’ extracts based on their functional properties. This study corroborates the therapeutic efficacy of local plants and highlights their ecological and medicinal value as natural resources of phytogenic compounds that have not yet fully explored and characterized and thus, can be utilized in various pharmaceutical products using modern scientific analytical techniques.
